name: La Hoz del Rio Mijares – circular until the Barranco de Linares
name_original: La Hoz del Río Mijares – circular hasta el Barranco de Linares
region_intro: Hiking around Olba, a municipality in Teruel, Spain, offers tranquil outdoor experiences characterized by riverine landscapes and wooded areas. The region features the Mijares and Rubielos Rivers, which flow through picturesque gorges and provide varied terrain for exploration. Natural springs, such as Fuente de la Salud, are integrated into accessible routes, enhancing the natural appeal of the area. Best hiking trails around Olba The most popular hiking route is La Hoz del Rio Mijares – circular until the Barranco de Linares, an easy 3.0 miles (4.8 km) trail that takes 1 hour 26 minutes to complete. This route follows the Mijares River through a wooded area, passing by natural springs. Another top favourite among local hikers is Aceicuca Trail, an easy 3.2 miles (5.1 km) path. This trail offers a straightforward walk through the varied natural landscapes of Olba. Local hikers also love the Circular route to Morrón de Olba, a 5.5 miles (8.9 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 2 hours 45 minutes. Hiking around Olba is defined by riverine landscapes, gorges, and wooded areas. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to more challenging hikes. The routes in Olba are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 160 reviews. More than 1,000 hikers have used komoot to explore Olba's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Olba?
A: Olba offers a selection of over 20 hiking routes, catering to various preferences and skill levels. These trails explore the tranquil riverine landscapes and wooded areas characteristic of the region.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when hiking in Olba?
A: Hiking in Olba features diverse terrain, primarily defined by the picturesque Mijares and Rubielos Rivers. You'll encounter wooded areas, narrow gorges, and varied natural landscapes, offering a tranquil outdoor experience.

Q: Are there hiking options for different skill levels in Olba?
A: Yes, Olba provides trails for all abilities. There are 4 easy routes, 15 moderate routes, and 3 more challenging options, ensuring a suitable path for every hiker.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Olba?
A: The trails in Olba are highly regarded by the komoot community, boasting an average rating of 4.6 stars from over 160 reviews. Hikers often praise the quiet natural beauty and the well-maintained paths along the rivers.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Olba?
A: Yes, Olba features several circular routes. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e981480458/circular-route-to-morron-de-olba" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Circular route to Morrón de Olba&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 5.5-mile (8.9 km) trail that takes about 2 hours 45 minutes to complete.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while hiking in Olba?
A: While hiking in Olba, you can explore several natural features. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5646525" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Estrechos de Chillapájaros&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4785172" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mijares River Narrows&lt;/a&gt; offer impressive gorge landscapes. You might also encounter the charming Fuente de la Salud, a natural spring integrated into local routes.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking trails in Olba?
A: Yes, Olba offers family-friendly options. Easy trails like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e981485482/la-hoz-del-rio-mijares-circular-until-the-barranco-de-linares" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Hoz del Rio Mijares – circular until the Barranco de Linares&lt;/a&gt;, which passes by natural springs, are suitable for families looking for a gentle outdoor experience.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the trails in Olba?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on hiking trails in Olba. It's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or near other hikers, and to clean up after them to preserve the natural environment.

Q: When is the best time of year to go hiking in Olba?
A: The best time for hiking in Olba is typically during spring and autumn when the weather is mild and pleasant. These seasons offer comfortable temperatures for exploring the riverine landscapes and wooded areas.

Q: Are there opportunities for wild swimming near the hiking trails in Olba?
A: The Mijares and Rubielos Rivers, which flow through Olba, offer natural water access. While not explicitly designated swimming spots, these riverine landscapes may provide opportunities for refreshing dips, especially during warmer months.

Q: Are there any viewpoints or peaks to discover in Olba?
A: Yes, the region around Olba features several viewpoints and peaks. You can find highlights such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4416522" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico de Santa Bárbara and Chapel of San Gregorio&lt;/a&gt;, or enjoy the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3566669" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Embalse de Arenoso and Muela de Mijares from El Morrón de Campos&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is parking available at trailheads in Olba?
A: While specific parking details for every trailhead are not provided, Olba is a rural municipality, and parking is generally available in or near the village centers and at common starting points for trails. It is advisable to check local signage upon arrival.
tips: There is a hiking route called “Las Hoces del Mijares” that you can explore. It is approximately 10.24 km long and offers stunning views.

You cannot access the plant or its bridge, but you can enjoy the river.
Viewpoint over the ravine, at the height of the hydroelectric power plant.

The Barranco de Linares offers stunning views and is a popular destination for hiking enthusiasts. One of the most recommended routes is the circular route that passes through the Hoz del Río Mijares and reaches the Barranco de Linares.
The Devil's Bridge designation refers to several dozen ancient bridges that, according to popular tradition, would have been built by the Devil, with his help, or even against his wishes.

waypoints.name: Small reservoir next to a hydroelectric power station
